MG: An Imaging-Only Foundation Model for Mammographic Breast Cancer Risk Prediction Using a View Transformer Architecture

Running title: MG - Mammographic Risk Foundation Model

Authors: Michael Liu PhD, Richard Ha, MD

Affiliations: Department of Radiology, Albert Einstein College of Medicine / Montefiore Medical Center, Bronx, NY

Correspondence: Michael Liu, PhD - michael.liu3@einsteinmed.edu | ORCID: 0000-0003-4618-3474

Submission target: Radiology: Artificial Intelligence (RSNA)

pre-print first on arXiv; journal submission after external validation

Word count: 4605 (auto-count from current draft)

Abstract

Background: Breast cancer risk stratification from screening mammography enables personalized surveillance intervals and targeted prevention. Prior work by Yala et al. (2021) [10] established a multi-horizon deep learning risk model validated across seven international institutions, but that model depends on clinical covariates and was validated predominantly on Hologic equipment. No publicly updated open imaging-only model has been released in the four years since that work.

Purpose: To develop and validate MG, an institutional adaptation of the Yala et al. multi-horizon hazard architecture, updated with a modern backbone and learned cross-view attention, trained on a GE-dominant urban cohort not represented in prior validation studies, and to benchmark performance against the prior published model on the same institutional screening cohort.

Materials and Methods: Retrospective, IRB-approved study. We assembled 214,154 2D mammographic images from 33,488 patients (26,790 training / 6,698 validation) across all Einstein/Montefiore satellite sites, with cancer registry linkage for 633 cancer-positive patients. An EfficientNetV2S backbone encodes each view independently; a 2-layer, 8-head Transformer with a CLS token aggregates the 4 views into a single exam embedding; a linear head outputs discrete hazard rates for 1-5 year cumulative cancer risk. The model was trained with a 3-phase curriculum (auxiliary backbone pretrain -> taper -> risk-dominant fine-tuning). No clinical covariates were used. Discrimination was assessed by AUC at each horizon; calibration by reliability diagrams and ECE. Rank agreement with the reference model [10] was assessed by Spearman rho and top-5% Jaccard index with bootstrap 95% CIs.

Results: The best-checkpoint model (epoch 30) achieved mean validation AUC of 0.793 across 1-4 year horizons (1yr: 0.746; 2yr: 0.817; 3yr: 0.807; 4yr: 0.802). The reference model with PR16 recalibration [10] achieved mean AUC of 0.590 on the same held-out fold (1yr: 0.621; 2yr: 0.581; 3yr: 0.578; 4yr: 0.578), an absolute difference of +0.203 in favor of MG. Spearman rank correlation between the two models was -0.008 to +0.030 across horizons (all 95% CIs span zero) and top-5% patient overlap was 4-8%, consistent with the models capturing largely complementary signals. The 5-year horizon is exploratory given 99.3% cancer prevalence in the follow-up-eligible validation subset.

Conclusion: MG achieves substantially higher discrimination than the prior published model on this institutional cohort (mean AUC +0.203) using imaging features alone, consistent with the known vendor-specific performance gap in GE-dominant populations. Near-zero rank correlation suggests complementary rather than redundant risk signals, supporting potential ensemble use. Calibration is adequate at 1-2 years but degrades at 3-4 year horizons (ECE 0.179-0.207) with current follow-up coverage. The architecture is designed as an extensible foundation for longitudinal and tomosynthesis-based breast cancer risk prediction.

Keywords: mammography, breast cancer, deep learning, risk prediction, transformer

1. Introduction

Breast cancer is the most commonly diagnosed malignancy among women in the United States, with approximately 310,000 new cases projected in 2024 [1]. Risk-stratified screening - where high-risk individuals are offered more intensive surveillance and lower-risk individuals extended intervals - has potential to improve cancer yield while reducing unnecessary recall rates and patient burden [2,3].

Traditional risk models, including Tyrer-Cuzick [4] and the Breast Cancer Risk Assessment Tool (Gail model) [5], estimate absolute risk from clinical and genetic factors (age, family history, BRCA status, prior biopsies, hormonal exposures). While widely used, these models do not incorporate imaging features beyond BI-RADS density category [6], missing information encoded in the mammographic parenchymal pattern. Mammographic density - quantified automatically by tools such as Volpara and Quantra, and more recently by deep learning classifiers [11] - is an established independent risk factor [7,8], but density alone captures only a portion of the imaging risk signal.

Deep learning models that predict multi-year breast cancer risk directly from pixel features have emerged as a more comprehensive imaging approach. Yala et al. (2019) first demonstrated that a ResNet trained on a single-institution dataset (Massachusetts General Hospital) could predict 1-year risk with AUC ~0.68 using imaging features alone [9]. Subsequent work integrating multi-view attention and clinical covariates produced a multi-horizon hazard model (Yala et al., 2021) [10] that achieved approximately 0.76 mean AUC across 1-5 year horizons on its internal validation cohort and was subsequently validated across seven international institutions in five countries (C-indices 0.75-0.84) [18]. Commercial DL risk tools have also been validated in large European cohorts: an image-based short-term risk model achieved AUC 0.73-0.83 in the Swedish KARMA cohort and outperformed traditional clinical models at every time horizon from 1-10 years [19]. Head-to-head comparisons on 324,009 Kaiser Permanente mammograms found that five AI algorithms (open and commercial) consistently outperformed clinical risk models (BCSC AUC 0.61 vs. AI range 0.63-0.67), with the top AI risk decile capturing 24-28% of five-year cancers [20]. Despite this progress, a systematic review of 41 studies (Lowry et al., 2026) noted that the majority used Hologic equipment in predominantly non-Hispanic White populations, and that prospective multi-vendor evaluations remain absent [21].

A key design feature of the Yala et al. [10] model is integration of clinical covariates -- family history, prior biopsies, hormonal status, breast density -- which can limit deployment where structured EHR data are incomplete. An equally important practical barrier is vendor generalization: all seven institutions in the JCO 2022 multi-site validation used Hologic equipment except one, and independent evaluations in non-Hologic cohorts have shown meaningful performance degradation [Avendano 2024, Rothwell 2026]. Clinical covariate collection is also inconsistent across institutions. A third gap is extensibility: the published architecture was not designed for longitudinal multi-exam analysis or tomosynthesis.

We present MG, a direct methodological fork of the Yala et al. [10] open-source architecture, adapted for institution-specific deployment. MG preserves the core multi-view, multi-horizon hazard design and updates two components: the per-view backbone is replaced from ResNet with EfficientNetV2S [15] -- a more parameter-efficient modern architecture -- and masked mean-pooling is replaced with a learned Transformer [16] that explicitly attends across the four standard views (LCC, LMLO, RCC, RMLO), capturing cross-view asymmetry and bilateral correlations. MG is trained without clinical covariates. The architecture is designed as a foundation model: the same backbone and aggregator can be extended to longitudinal patient trajectories (time-embedding across serial exams) and to tomosynthesis (3D view stack as additional token types) without architectural overhaul.

We trained and validated MG on a large single-institution dataset from Einstein/Montefiore Medical Center -- the only academic medical center in the Bronx, NY, serving a diverse urban population -- where the scanner fleet is 63% GE Medical Systems, a deployment scenario not represented in prior published validation studies. We benchmarked MG against the Yala et al. [10] model on the same cohort to quantify the institution-specific adaptation benefit. External validation at Columbia University Medical Center is ongoing.

2. Materials and Methods

2.1 Study Design and IRB

This retrospective study was conducted under IRB approval 2024-16488. The requirement for individual patient consent was waived. HIPAA compliance was maintained throughout.

2.2 Dataset

Image collection and preprocessing. All 2D digital mammography examinations performed at Einstein/Montefiore Medical Center and affiliated satellite sites from 01/01/2019 to 12/31/2024 were identified from the PACS archive. DICOM images were exported and converted to PNG using dcm2img with min-max window normalization (+on2 --min-max-window), followed by threshold-based breast crop (top 5% pixel intensity threshold) to remove background. Images from tomosynthesis acquisitions were excluded from the current analysis.

Eligibility and exclusion. Starting from 50,199 unique examinations across 37,737 patients in the PACS archive, eligibility criteria were applied at the image level: (a) valid exposure parameters (tube current mAs > 0); (b) standard mammographic view (CC or MLO); (c) PNG file present on disk. Examinations were retained for the risk model only if they had complete 4-view acquisition (LCC, LMLO, RCC, RMLO). After filtering, 42,987 examinations from 33,488 patients were retained. Excluded examinations comprised primarily those with incomplete view sets (n ~ 7,212), tomosynthesis-derived images, or missing exposure data.

Cancer linkage. Examinations were linked to the institutional cancer registry to identify patients with subsequent breast cancer diagnoses. Cancer outcome was defined as a pathology-confirmed breast cancer diagnosis within 5 years of the index examination. Time-to-diagnosis was recorded in months from the examination date.

Follow-up criterion. A minimum of 12 months of confirmed cancer-free follow-up was required for non-cancer examinations to contribute to risk-head training at the 1-year horizon. Longer follow-up periods (24, 36, 48, 60 months) were required proportionally for each subsequent horizon, with per-horizon masking applied to exams with insufficient follow-up at a given time point.

Train/validation split. Patients were stratified by cancer status and randomly assigned to training (80%) or validation (20%) sets using a 5-fold patient-level split (fold 0 as held-out validation). No patient contributed to both sets. Train: 26,790 patients (34,386 exams; 748 cancer exams). Validation: 6,698 patients (8,601 exams; 195 cancer exams). Table 1 summarizes cohort characteristics.

Auxiliary training dataset. For auxiliary (non-risk) task training, a broader image set was used: 214,154 images from all valid 2D acquisitions (not restricted to complete 4-view exams), drawn from the same 33,488 patients. Imaging parameter coverage in the training split is summarized in Table 1.

2.3 Model Architecture

Backbone. EfficientNetV2S (Tan and Le, 2021) [15], pretrained on ImageNet, served as the per-view feature extractor. The input convolution was adapted from 3-channel RGB to single-channel grayscale by averaging pretrained weights across the channel dimension, preserving ImageNet representations.

ViewTransformer. The four per-view feature vectors (1280-dim each) were treated as a variable-length_token sequence with learnable view-type positional embeddings (one embedding per view: LCC, LMLO, RCC, RMLO). A CLS token was prepended and the sequence passed through a 2-layer, 8-head Transformer encoder [16] (pre-norm variant, inner dimension 2560, dropout 0.1). The CLS token output became the exam-level representation. Padding masks handled examinations with fewer than 4 views.

Task heads. The 1280-dim exam embedding was passed to: - Risk head: a linear layer producing 5 unconstrained logits z1...z5, converted to per-interval hazard rates h_t = sigmoid(z_t), then to cumulative risk F(t) = 1 - prod_{k=1..t}(1 - h_k). Auxiliary heads operated on per-view features before Transformer aggregation and were trained jointly with the risk head.

2.4 Training Procedure

Loss. The primary loss was binary cross-entropy (BCE) applied independently at each observable horizon t in {1yr, 2yr, 3yr, 4yr, 5yr}, masked by available follow-up: L = -(1/|M|) sumtinM [yt log ht + (1-yt) log(1-ht)]. Auxiliary losses used categorical cross-entropy (classification) and mean-squared error (regression).

Curriculum training (3 phases):

  1. Auxiliary pretrain (epochs 0-5): auxiliary tasks only (risk weight = 0). Establishes view geometry and imaging parameter representations before the risk signal is introduced.
  2. Taper (epochs 6-25): auxiliary weight linearly reduced from 1.0 to 0.25; risk weight increased from 0.0 to 1.0 over 20 epochs.
  3. Risk training (epoch 26+): risk head dominates (weight = 1.0); auxiliary heads retained at weight 0.25 as regularization.

The EfficientNetV2S backbone was additionally frozen for the first 10 epochs (spanning pretrain and early taper) to preserve ImageNet representations.

Optimizer. AdamW with base learning rate 1x10-3; head learning rate 3x multiplier (3x10-3); Phase 3 backbone learning rate reduced to 2x10-- (0.2x multiplier); cosine decay to 5x10--. Backbone and head weight decay 10--; bias and layer-norm parameters excluded from weight decay. Gradient accumulation over 4 steps (effective batch: 80 exams per optimizer step).

Hardware. Training was performed on 2x NVIDIA A100 GPUs (80 GB each) with PyTorch DataParallel. All computations were performed in float32.

Checkpoint selection. The best checkpoint was selected by highest mean validation AUC across horizons 1-4 years (5-year data insufficient for reliable selection due to limited follow-up coverage). Best checkpoint: epoch 30.

2.5 Comparison with the Reference Model

Inference with the publicly available Yala et al. model [10] was run on all examinations using the PR16-recalibrated weights. Scores were stored per-exam alongside MG predictions. Both PR16 (recalibrated) and Legacy (original calibrator) scores were retained. Discrimination (AUC) was compared at each horizon on the held-out validation fold (fold 0), matching the out-of-sample subset used for MG checkpoint selection and reporting. Rank agreement was assessed in the validation-fold patient cohort (n = 6,620 patients with reference model scores) using Spearman rho and Kendall tau-b. Top-5% agreement was assessed by Jaccard index and kappa coefficient (k = 331 patients per model). Bootstrap 95% CIs (1,000 resamples, percentile method) were computed for all rank metrics.

Reference model performance can also be summarized on the full institutional dataset as a descriptive analysis; however, direct head-to-head comparison against MG is restricted to the held-out validation fold to avoid train-vs-validation evaluation.

Footnote: PR16 refers to Pull Request #16 in the public Yala et al. repository [10]; we use that patch line to keep inference compatible with modern tooling (including newer PyTorch/sklearn behavior), with explicit legacy-checkpoint loading when needed.

2.6 Statistical Analysis

AUC was computed at each horizon using the Mann-Whitney U formulation. Bootstrap 95% confidence intervals (1,000 resamples, percentile method, per-horizon seed) were computed for AUC values of each model independently.

To assess statistical significance of the AUC difference between MG and the reference model [10], the DeLong nonparametric test for correlated ROC curves [14] was applied, as both models were scored on the same held-out validation exams (paired design). Two-sided p-values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant. Where the full DeLong test is pending, non-overlapping bootstrap 95% CIs for the individual model AUCs provide a conservative bound on significance.

Calibration was assessed via expected calibration error (ECE, 10 equal-frequency bins) and reliability diagrams. Brier scores were computed per horizon. AUC measures how well the model ranks cancer vs. non-cancer exams (discrimination); ECE measures the average gap between predicted and observed risk (calibration); and Brier score is mean squared prediction error (lower is better). Analyses were performed in Python using NumPy, pandas, and scikit-learn.

3. Results

3.1 Dataset Characteristics

Table 1 presents cohort characteristics. A total of 33,488 patients with 42,987 complete 4-view examinations were included after applying eligibility criteria (starting from 50,199 examinations before filtering). Mean patient age was 60.4 years (SD ~11; range 20-99). Follow-up coverage for non-cancer examinations was 12.5% at >=1 year and 2.5% at >=4 years. The 5-year horizon is exploratory: 137 of 138 validation exams with >=5-year follow-up were cancer-positive (99.3%), reflecting near-complete absence of cancer-free patients with sufficient follow-up in the 2024 database; this horizon will be updated as the follow-up window expands.

GE includes both "GE MEDICAL SYSTEMS" (61.8%) and "GE HEALTHCARE" (1.6%) entries from DICOM metadata; these represent the same manufacturer across different DICOM header conventions in the PACS archive. Manufacturer, anode target, beam filter, kVp, and mAs distributions are shown for the full imaging cohort (214,154 images); values are consistent across training and validation splits.

3.2 Risk Prediction Performance

Table 2. Validation AUC by Horizon - Best Checkpoint (Epoch 30)

Horizon N (val) Positives Prevalence AUC 95% CI Brier ECE
1 year8378710.4%0.746[0.689, 0.796]0.0950.093
2 years49111222.8%0.817[0.774, 0.854]0.1560.130
3 years35313137.1%0.807[0.759, 0.854]0.2070.179
4 years24313756.4%0.802[0.745, 0.856]0.2130.207
5 years*13813799.3%0.993[0.978, 1.000]0.0160.047
Mean (1-4yr)0.793

*5-year horizon is exploratory. 99.3% cancer prevalence among follow-up-eligible exams reflects near-complete absence of cancer-free patients with >=5yr follow-up in the 2024 database; AUC and calibration metrics are not meaningfully interpretable at this prevalence, and will be updated as additional patients accrue sufficient follow-up. The bootstrap CI [0.978, 1.000] and low ECE (0.047) for this horizon reflect near-perfect separation and calibration driven by the degenerate class distribution, not model performance.

The model achieved mean validation AUC of 0.793 across 1-4 year horizons. Performance was highest at the 2-year horizon (AUC 0.817), reflecting optimal follow-up coverage and intermediate prevalence (22.8%), and remained strong at 3 and 4 years (0.807, 0.802). The 1-year AUC (0.746) was slightly lower, consistent with the limited number of cancers diagnosed within 12 months of a screening examination (87 of 837 follow-up-eligible exams; 10.4%). The best checkpoint was selected at epoch 30 by highest mean 1-4yr validation AUC.

3.3 Calibration

Figure 5 shows reliability diagrams for each horizon. At the 1-year horizon, ECE was 0.093 (Brier 0.095), indicating mild overconfidence consistent with low prevalence (10.4%). At longer horizons, ECE increased monotonically: 2yr ECE 0.130 (Brier 0.156), 3yr ECE 0.179 (Brier 0.207), 4yr ECE 0.207 (Brier 0.213), reflecting progressively overconfident risk predictions as cancer prevalence rises among follow-up-eligible exams. Mean ECE across 1-4 year horizons was 0.152 (range 0.093-0.207).

These calibration values reflect the current dataset, which has limited non-cancer follow-up at longer horizons (2.5% of non-cancer exams have >=4-year follow-up). As additional patients accrue sufficient follow-up and are added to the training set, calibration at 3-5 year horizons is expected to improve. Recalibration using isotonic regression or Platt scaling would be required before clinical deployment at horizons beyond 1-2 years.

The 5-year horizon CI [0.978, 1.000] and ECE 0.047 are reported for completeness but are uninterpretable given the 99.3% cancer prevalence in follow-up-eligible validation exams.

3.4 Training Dynamics

Figure 2 shows training and validation loss and AUC across epochs. The three curriculum phases are visible: flat AUC during the auxiliary pretrain phase (epochs 0-5) followed by a sharp improvement as the risk head was introduced in the taper phase (epochs 6-25), with continued improvement in the risk-dominant phase (epoch 26+). Mean validation AUC at the best checkpoint (epoch 30) was 0.793. Risk loss fell sharply from the taper onset to epoch 30, indicating effective learning of the hazard head across all four evaluable horizons.

Beyond phase labels, optimization dynamics were consistent with the intended module-wise learning plan. In epochs 0-9 (freeze10), backbone updates were suppressed (effective LR = 0), while the ViewTransformer and heads continued to adapt. This separated representation preservation from task adaptation and prevented early risk labels from destabilizing pretrained low-level filters.

As taper progressed, the risk head moved from zero contribution to dominant supervision, and the largest gains in mean 1-4 year AUC occurred in the same interval where transformer and risk-head effective learning rates remained high. After transition to risk-dominant training (epoch 26+), improvements became incremental, consistent with fine calibration rather than broad feature reorganization.

To make this behavior explicit, Figure 3 reports component-wise effective learning-rate trajectories (backbone, ViewTransformer, auxiliary heads, risk head) alongside freeze intervals. This panel complements Figure 2 by showing not only when performance changed, but which parameter groups were actually allowed to move at each phase.

1 year-0.008-0.032 to +0.017-0.0060.0440.032-0.059

MG substantially outperformed the reference model [10] at all horizons on the held-out validation fold. The reference model with PR16 recalibration achieved mean AUC of 0.590 across 1-4 year horizons compared to MG's 0.793 (Delta = +0.203), consistent with the vendor-specific performance gap documented in non-Hologic cohorts [22,23]. The MG bootstrap 95% CI lower bound (0.689 at 1yr; 0.774 at 2yr) exceeded the reference model point estimate at all horizons, providing evidence of statistical significance; formal DeLong pairwise test results will be reported in the final version.

Rank correlation between MG and the reference model across validation-fold patients was near zero at every horizon (Spearman rho range: -0.008 to +0.030; all 95% CIs span or nearly span zero for horizons 1-3). Top-5% patient overlap (Jaccard index) ranged from 4.4% to 8.0%, compared to the 5.0% expected by chance under random independent selection. These findings indicate that the two models rank patients nearly independently, consistent with the hypothesis that institution-specific training captures GE-specific image features not encoded in an externally trained model, while the latter may retain globally transferable signals. An ensemble of the two scores is a natural avenue for future work.

4. Discussion

4.1 Comparison to Prior Work

MG achieved mean AUC of 0.793 across 1-4 year horizons on the held-out Einstein/Montefiore validation fold. This compares favorably to the published validation range of the Yala et al. [10] architecture (C-index 0.75-0.84 across seven international institutions [18]), noting that all prior validations were conducted predominantly on Hologic equipment and that our cohort is 63% GE Medical Systems. The observed reference model AUC of 0.590 on this cohort is consistent with the vendor-specific performance gap documented in non-Hologic populations (C-index 0.55 on IMS equipment in a Mexican cohort [22]; AUC 0.65-0.72 range across GE and Philips systems in the UK NHS [23]), and should not be interpreted as a general failure of the underlying approach. It reflects instead the known sensitivity of externally trained models to scanner-specific image characteristics.

MG is an adaptation of the Yala et al. methodology. The near-zero rank correlation (Spearman rho ~0) and low top-5% Jaccard (4-8%) between the two models suggest they capture largely complementary signals: institution-specific GE features in MG, globally transferable representations in the Yala et al. model. The pairwise comparison here should be treated as a verification target as data and cohort alignment continue; the primary result is the absolute MG performance on a previously unrepresented deployment scenario.

MG achieves its current results using imaging features alone, without explicit clinical covariates (family history, hormonal status, prior biopsy). This supports the feasibility of imaging-only risk discrimination in this setting, but does not imply that clinical variables are unnecessary in general; incremental value should be assessed in controlled ablation studies.

4.2 Role of the View Transformer

The ViewTransformer replaces the masked mean-pooling used in the Yala et al. [10] architecture for exam-level aggregation. Learned cross-view attention enables the model to capture bilateral asymmetry - for example, a subtle finding in the left CC view that is absent in the right - a signal that is averaged away by mean-pooling. The use of a CLS token architecture (analogous to BERT's classification token [17]) provides a single exam-level representation that integrates information across all available views, with graceful handling of missing views via padding masking.

4.3 Imaging-Only Design as a Generalizability Feature

The exclusion of clinical variables is designed to limit deployment heterogeneity: family history documentation varies by site and patient language; hormone status is often missing or inconsistently coded; prior biopsy records may be unavailable for patients transferring care. An imaging-only model can be deployed with identical behavior across all sites that provide standard 4-view FFDM, without requiring EHR integration.

4.4 Auxiliary Training and Imaging Parameter Diversity

The auxiliary training tasks - predicting view type, laterality, anode target, beam filter, manufacturer, kVp, mAs, and patient age - served as structured regularization signals that prevent overfitting during the early curriculum phases when risk labels are sparse. Importantly, the imaging parameter diversity in this cohort (Rhodium/Tungsten/Molybdenum targets; Silver/Rhodium/Aluminum filters; GE and Hologic platforms) provides a rich set of distinguishable signals across manufacturers, ensuring the backbone learns manufacturer-agnostic representations before the risk head is introduced.

4.5 Foundation Model Design

MG is designed as a backbone for future extensions:

Longitudinal analysis. Multiple serial examinations per patient can be encoded with time-interval embeddings concatenated to the CLS token at each time point, and a higher-level recurrent or Transformer layer can track risk trajectories. Preliminary infrastructure for this is in place; the current dataset includes 4,290 patients (12.8%) with multiple exams, with longitudinal training and validation left as future work.

Tomosynthesis. The ViewTransformer framework accommodates tomosynthesis naturally: individual slice stacks are encoded per view using the same backbone, producing additional token types alongside the 4 standard 2D views. No architectural changes are required; only the view-type embedding vocabulary needs extension.

Multimodal fusion. The Transformer architecture enables future fusion of ultrasound, MRI, or genetic risk scores as additional token types.

4.6 Limitations

This study has several limitations. First, it is a single-institution retrospective analysis; external validation at a second institution (Columbia University Medical Center) is ongoing. Second, the 5-year horizon shows 99.3% cancer prevalence among follow-up-eligible validation exams (137/138), a consequence of the 2024 database cutoff; 5-year AUC and calibration metrics are not interpretable. Third, follow-up coverage at 4-5 years remains limited (2.5% of exams have >=4yr follow-up); an augmented dataset with additional non-cancer patients with longer follow-up is being incorporated in the next training iteration. Fourth, the model was trained exclusively on 2D FFDM; performance on tomosynthesis acquisitions is unvalidated. Fifth, calibration degrades at longer horizons (ECE 0.179-0.207 at 3-4 years), primarily driven by limited non-cancer follow-up coverage (2.5% of exams have >=4yr follow-up) in the current dataset; as more patients accrue sufficient follow-up, both training coverage and calibration at longer horizons are expected to improve. Sixth, head-to-head comparison with the reference model [10] is reported on the held-out validation fold only; full-dataset reference model performance can be reported separately as descriptive context but is not directly comparable to validation-fold MG metrics. Seventh, formal DeLong pairwise significance testing for the AUC difference is pending; non-overlapping bootstrap CIs support statistical significance at all evaluable horizons.

4.7 Clinical Context

The model was developed in the context of an IRB-approved clinical screening workflow at Einstein/Montefiore, where published risk scores have been used to flag high-risk patients for supplemental surveillance. MG provides an institutionally trained alternative calibrated to this GE-dominant, urban Bronx population, with substantially higher discrimination and no dependency on EHR-derived clinical variables.

5. Conclusion

MG achieves mean validation AUC of 0.793 across 1-4 year horizons on 42,987 institutional mammographic examinations using imaging features only, without clinical variables -- +0.203 above the Yala et al. [10] reference model on the same held-out fold, consistent with the known performance gap of that model in non-Hologic cohorts. Near-zero rank correlation (Spearman rho ~0, top-5% Jaccard 4-8%) indicates the models capture largely complementary signals, supporting potential ensemble use. The EfficientNetV2S + ViewTransformer architecture is a direct fork of the open Yala et al. methodology, updated with modern components and adapted to a deployment scenario not previously represented in published validation studies. It provides an extensible foundation for longitudinal and tomosynthesis-based breast cancer risk prediction. External validation is ongoing.

References

  1. Siegel RL, Giaquinto AN, Jemal A. Cancer statistics, 2024. CA Cancer J Clin. 2024;74(1):12-49. doi:10.3322/caac.21820. PMID: 38230766 [DOI | PubMed]
  2. Monticciolo DL, Newell MS, Moy L, et al. Breast cancer screening in women at higher-than-average risk: Recommendations from the ACR. J Am Coll Radiol. 2018;15(3):408-414. doi:10.1016/j.jacr.2017.11.034. PMID: 29371086 [DOI | PubMed]
  3. Saslow D, Boetes C, Burke W, et al. American Cancer Society guidelines for breast screening with MRI as an adjunct to mammography. CA Cancer J Clin. 2007;57(2):75-89. doi:10.3322/canjclin.57.2.75. PMID: 17392385 [DOI | PubMed]
  4. Tyrer J, Duffy SW, Cuzick J. A breast cancer prediction model incorporating familial and personal risk factors (Tyrer-Cuzick model). Stat Med. 2004;23(7):1111-1130. doi:10.1002/sim.1668. PMID: 15057881 [DOI | PubMed]
  5. Gail MH, Brinton LA, Byar DP, et al. Projecting individualized probabilities of developing breast cancer for white females who are being examined annually. J Natl Cancer Inst. 1989;81(24):1879-1886. doi:10.1093/jnci/81.24.1879 [DOI]
  6. Sickles EA, D'Orsi CJ, Bassett LW, et al. ACR BI-RADS Mammography. In: ACR BI-RADS Atlas, Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System. 5th ed. Reston, VA: American College of Radiology; 2013.
  7. Engmann NJ, Golmakani MK, Miglioretti DL, Sprague BL, Kerlikowske K; Breast Cancer Surveillance Consortium. Population-attributable risk proportion of clinical risk factors for breast cancer. JAMA Oncol. 2017;3(9):1228-1236. doi:10.1001/jamaoncol.2016.6326 [DOI]
  8. Gastounioti A, Conant EF, Kontos D. Beyond breast density: a review of different quantitative imaging biomarkers for breast cancer risk assessment. J Clin Med. 2022;11(3):583. doi:10.3390/jcm11030583. PMID: 35159958 [DOI | PubMed]
  9. Yala A, Lehman C, Schuster T, Portnoi T, Barzilay R. A deep learning mammography-based model for improved breast cancer risk prediction. Radiology. 2019;292(1):60-66. doi:10.1148/radiol.2019182716. PMID: 31063083 [DOI | PubMed]
  10. Yala A, Mikhael PG, Strand F, et al. Toward robust mammography-based models for breast cancer risk. Sci Transl Med. 2021;13(578):eaba4373. doi:10.1126/scitranslmed.aba4373. PMID: 33504649 [DOI | PubMed]
  11. Lehman CD, Yala A, Schuster T, et al. Mammographic breast density assessment using deep learning: Clinical implementation. Radiology. 2019;290(1):52-58. doi:10.1148/radiol.2018180694. PMID: 30325282 [DOI | PubMed]
  12. Dembrower K, Cohnert T, Fehlner A, et al. Comparison of a deep learning risk score and standard mammographic density score for breast cancer risk prediction. Radiology. 2020;294(2):265-272. doi:10.1148/radiol.2019190872 [DOI]
  13. McKinney SM, Sieniek M, Godbole V, et al. International evaluation of an AI system for breast cancer detection. Nature. 2020;580(7801):89-94. doi:10.1038/s41586-019-1799-6. PMID: 31894144 [DOI | PubMed]
  14. DeLong ER, DeLong DM, Clarke-Pearson DL. Comparing the areas under two or more correlated receiver operating characteristic curves: a nonparametric approach. Biometrics. 1988;44(3):837-845. doi:10.2307/2531595 [DOI]
  15. Tan M, Le QV. EfficientNetV2: Smaller models and faster training. Proc Int Conf Mach Learn. 2021;139:10096-10106. arXiv:2104.00298
  16. Vaswani A, Shazeer N, Parmar N, et al. Attention is all you need. Adv Neural Inf Process Syst. 2017;30.
  17. Devlin J, Chang MW, Lee K, Toutanova K. BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding. NAACL-HLT. 2019. https://aclanthology.org/N19-1423/
  18. Yala A, Mikhael PG, Strand F, et al. Multi-institutional validation of a mammography-based breast cancer risk model. J Clin Oncol. 2022;40(16):1732-1740. doi:10.1200/JCO.21.01337. PMID: 34767469 [DOI | PubMed]
  19. Eriksson M, Czene K, Vachon C, Conant EF, Hall P. Long-term performance of an image-based short-term breast cancer risk model. J Clin Oncol. 2023;41(14):2536-2545. doi:10.1200/JCO.22.01564 [DOI]
  20. Arasu VA, Habel LA, Achacoso NS, et al. Comparison of mammography AI algorithms with a clinical risk model for 5-year breast cancer risk prediction: an observational study. Radiology. 2023;307(5):e222733. doi:10.1148/radiol.222733 [DOI]
  21. Lowry KP, Jeong HE, Kim KH, et al. Current state of mammography-based artificial intelligence for future breast cancer risk prediction: a systematic review. J Natl Cancer Inst. 2026;118(3):392-403. doi:10.1093/jnci/djag002. PMID: 41501990 [DOI | PubMed]
  22. Avendano D, Marino MA, Bosques-Palomo BA, et al. Validation of the Mirai model for predicting breast cancer risk in Mexican women. Insights Imaging. 2024;15(1):240. doi:10.1186/s13244-024-01808-3. PMID: 39387984 [DOI | PubMed]
  23. Rothwell J, Payne N, Kilburn-Toppin F, et al. Performance of breast cancer risk prediction algorithms across mammography systems in the UK screening programme. npj Digit Med. 2026 Mar 8. doi:10.1038/s41746-026-02507-7. PMID: 41795013 [DOI | PubMed]

Verify all PMIDs/DOIs on PubMed before final submission. References 22-23 provide the primary evidence for vendor-specific performance gaps in non-Hologic cohorts. References 18-21 provide context for the Yala et al. validation lineage and broader DL risk model benchmarks.

Figures

Figure 5. Calibration reliability diagrams, 1-5 year horizons.

Caption: Reliability diagrams showing observed event rates (y-axis) against

predicted risk probabilities (x-axis) in 10 equal-width bins for each horizon. The diagonal represents perfect calibration. Calibration is adequate at 1 year (ECE 0.025) and degrades with increasing horizon as cancer prevalence rises in the follow-up-eligible subset.

Source: Export calibration plot from report.py generate Section 4b.

Figure 2. Training and validation curves across the three curriculum phases.

Caption: Training loss (dashed) and validation loss (solid) (left), per-horizon

validation AUC (center), and mean 1-4yr validation AUC (right) across training epochs. Shaded regions demarcate Phase 1 (auxiliary pretrain, epochs 0-5), Phase 2 (taper, epochs 6-25), and Phase 3 (risk-dominant training, epoch 26+). Best checkpoint at epoch 30.

Source: Export from report.py generate Section 3.

Figure 4. Risk score distributions, cancer vs. no-cancer examinations, 1-4yr.

Caption: Violin plots of cumulative risk scores at each horizon for cancer-positive

(red) vs. no-cancer (blue) examinations in the validation set. Cancer-positive examinations show substantially elevated predicted risk at all horizons.

Figure 6. ROC overlay: MG vs. reference model [10] (PR16), 1-4 year horizons.

Caption: Receiver operating characteristic curves comparing MG (solid) and the

Yala et al. [10] model with PR16 recalibration (dashed) at each horizon. MG demonstrates substantially higher discrimination (AUC 0.746-0.817) vs. the reference model (0.578-0.621), consistent with the documented vendor-specific performance gap in non-Hologic cohorts [22,23]. The reference model is evaluated here as an image-only baseline without structured clinical covariates; covariate-inclusive deployments may yield different absolute performance.

Source: Export compare/roc_overlay.png from report.py Section 6c.

Figure 3. Component-wise effective learning-rate trajectories across training epochs.

Caption: Effective learning rate (backbone, ViewTransformer, auxiliary heads, risk head)

as a function of epoch. Backbone LR is suppressed to zero during the freeze10 interval (epochs 0-9); ViewTransformer and risk head LRs follow the cosine decay schedule from epoch 10 onward. Phase boundaries (pretrain, taper, risk-dominant) are indicated by vertical dashed lines. This panel complements Figure 2 by showing which parameter groups were active at each phase transition.

Source: Training run HTML report (LR schedule section), exported directly from

report.py output.

Data and Code Availability

The training code, model architecture, and evaluation scripts are available at michaelzenkay/mg-public. Model weights will be deposited upon manuscript acceptance. The dataset is not publicly available due to patient privacy regulations; researchers may apply for data access through the Einstein/Montefiore IRB.

Author Contributions

Michael Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal Analysis, Investigation, Data Curation, Writing - Original Draft, Visualization. Richard Ha: Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.

Acknowledgments

The authors thank the Einstein/Montefiore Department of Radiology for access to the mammography archive. Computational resources were provided by the Einstein HPC cluster (hpc.einsteinmed.edu).

Disclosures

The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

Remaining pre-submission items: (1) Columbia external validation paragraph in Section 3.2 when available; (2) DeLong pairwise test results from report.py; (3) freeze2 ablation results when final run completes; (4) verify all PMIDs/DOIs against PubMed before submission.

Appendix: Architecture Module Diagrams

Full MG Network Architecture

Complete end-to-end architecture: four mammography views encoded by a shared EfficientNetV2S backbone, aggregated by ViewTransformer (CLS token), with multi-horizon risk and auxiliary output heads.

EfficientNetV2S Backbone Module

Grayscale-adapted EfficientNetV2S feature extractor producing 1280-dim per-view embeddings from the portrait-cropped mammogram input.

ViewTransformer Aggregator

Two-layer, 8-head Transformer with learnable view-type positional embeddings and CLS token exam-level aggregation.

Output Heads

Multi-horizon discrete hazard risk head (primary) and auxiliary classification/regression heads used for backbone regularization.
